text: During laparoscopy, dense intra-abdominal adhesions are visible, and the surgeon carefully inserts the remaining ports under direct vision.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis using scissors and a harmonic scalpel, carefully separating the small bowel from the abdominal wall where it is adhered.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to carefully dissect through omental adhesions, maintaining a bloodless surgical plane.
The surgeon uses scissors to carefully dissect the bowel that is adhered to the anterior abdominal wall, taking extreme care to avoid creating any enterotomies.
A hernial sac becomes visible on the right side of the midline, containing multiple loops of small bowel within it.
The surgeon carefully divides band adhesions holding the small bowel loops within the hernia sac, allowing the loops to be returned to the peritoneal cavity.
At the edge of the hernia sac, small bowel loops are visibly densely adherent to a mesh that was previously placed for hernia repair.
The surgeon continues to carefully dissect the adherent small bowel loops from the anterior abdominal wall, taking precautions to avoid inadvertent enterotomy during the procedure.
A second hernial defect, located suprapubically, becomes visible on the left side of the screen.

The surgeon encounters further densely adherent small bowel loops that cannot be safely freed using techniques such as hydrodissection. The decision is made to convert to an open procedure, allowing for the safe dissection and removal of the small bowel loops from the hernial sacs, which are then excised.
A 20 by 30 centimeter physio mesh is inserted into the peritoneal cavity, visible within the surgical field.
Stay sutures are inserted into the mesh and grafted using the endoclaw device. The sutures are pulled taut to lift the mesh onto the anterior abdominal wall.
The surgeon uses a secure strap device to circumferentially tack the mesh in place on the anterior abdominal wall.
Transfacial sutures are passed through the abdominal wall and mesh, anchoring the mesh securely in place as they are tied tightly.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the anterior abdominal wall by repeating the tacking process at five-centimeter intervals around its circumference, ensuring the mesh is held firmly in place.

The surgeon performs a thorough check for hemostasis, ensuring no bleeding is present. The bowel is meticulously inspected for any enterotomies or significant serosal tears. All surgical ports are then removed under direct vision.
